## Simulation

| Day | Price A | Price B | Note |
|-----|---------|---------|------|
| 0 | $4.00 | $4.00 | Start |
| 1 | $3.95 | $3.95 | Undercut |
| 2 | $3.90 | $3.90 | |
| 3 | $3.85 | $3.85 | |
| 4 | $3.80 | $3.80 | |
| 5 | $3.75 | $3.75 | |
| 6 | $3.70 | $3.70 | |
| 7 | $3.65 | $3.65 | |
| 8 | $3.60 | $3.60 | |
| 9 | $3.55 | $3.55 | |
| 10 | $3.50 | $3.50 | **Floor hit** |
| 11 | $3.60 | $3.60 | **Rebound +$0.10** |
| 12 | $3.55 | $3.55 | Undercut resumes |
| 13 | $3.50 | $3.50 | Floor hit |
| 14 | $3.60 | $3.60 | Rebound |
| 15 | $3.55 | $3.55 | |
| 16 | $3.50 | $3.50 | Floor hit |
| 17 | $3.60 | $3.60 | Rebound |
| 18 | $3.55 | $3.55 | |
| 19 | $3.50 | $3.50 | Floor hit |
| 20 | $3.60 | $3.60 | Rebound |

## Answer: **(D)**

The system locks into a **perpetual 3-day cycle**: $3.60 → $3.55 → $3.50 → $3.60 → ...

The key insight is that the $0.10 rebound is too small relative to the $0.05 undercut. It only takes **two rounds** of undercutting to return to the floor after each rebound. There is no mechanism in these rules that could ever push prices back above $3.60, so the analyst's prediction of $3.80 stabilization is wrong — prices pass through $3.80 once on the way down and never return.
